_comment: §7 AI diplomatic attitude & memory (Phase 7). attitude_base is the neutral starting score; live factors and the running memory total are summed onto it and clamped to 0..100, then mapped to an attitude level (0=furious .. 4=friendly) by attitude_thresholds (a level-i score is >= thresholds[i-1]). factors are computed live from current relations; memory_kinds accrue when a rival acts (Diplomacy.record adds `value`) and decay toward zero by `decay` points per turn (Diplomacy.decay). deal_accept_min_attitude / war_min_attitude gate AI behaviour. All integer math; provisional placeholders, not balance-tested.
_denial_comment: §7 denial reasons: why an AI refuses a trade offer (Diplomacy.evaluate_deal returns one id; '' = accepted). The refusal decision itself is unchanged — value >= 0 AND attitude >= deal_accept_min_attitude accepts — the reason names the most specific cause, checked in this order: no_trade_with_warring_party (proposer's alliance at war with ours and the offer carries no peace clause), worst_enemy (proposer is our lowest-scoring met rival at the furious level), attitude_too_low (attitude below deal_accept_min_attitude), tech_refusal (the offer asks techs off us and still values negative), insufficient_value (plain bad bargain). `text` is the display string surfaced by the trade notification and the diplomacy screen.

no_trade_with_warring_party.text: We do not bargain with a warring party. End this war first.

worst_enemy.text: We would sooner collapse than deal with our worst enemy.

attitude_too_low.text: Our relations are too poor to consider this.

tech_refusal.text: We will not part with our discoveries so cheaply.

insufficient_value.text: This offer is not worth enough to us.
